did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an improved golden jackal optimization algorithm (IGJO), a meta-heuristic computational method, to perform multilevel thresholding for image segmentation of skin cancer images. This segmentation is used to extract regions of interest to improve the classification process for skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a new algorithm (IGJO) for image segmentation and evaluates its performance through experimental results comparing it against seven other meta-heuristic algorithms using specific metrics (PSNR, SSIM, FSIM, MSE). It presents original experimental findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An efficient image segmentation method for skin cancer imaging using improved golden jackal optimization algorithm
brief_summary: This study addresses the critical need for early skin cancer diagnosis by proposing an efficient image segmentation method using an improved golden jackal optimization (IGJO) algorithm. The IGJO algorithm, which incorporates opposition-based learning, is applied to solve the multilevel thresholding problem using Otsu’s method as the objective function. The proposed approach is evaluated against seven other meta-heuristic algorithms, including whale optimization and salp swarm algorithms, to assess its segmentation performance. The evaluation utilizes four standard metrics: peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), structure similarity index (SSIM), feature similarity index (FSIM), and mean square error (MSE). Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed IGJO algorithm outperforms the compared alternative algorithms across all tested segmentation metrics. Consequently, the study concludes that the IGJO method effectively resolves skin cancer image segmentation issues, thereby supporting improved classification processes.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on skin cancer imaging and segmentation. It specifically mentions melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma as the three most prevalent types of skin cancer in the introduction, with melanoma highlighted as the most deadly type. The experiments involve segmenting skin cancer images generally, without specifying distinct lesion type labels for the segmentation task itself.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer images segmented into homogeneous sections or classes using multilevel thresholding.
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an improved golden jackal optimization (IGJO) algorithm to solve the multilevel thresholding problem for image segmentation. The goal is to extract regions of interest from skin cancer images to improve subsequent classification processes. The task involves grouping pixels with similar features into segments, evaluated by metrics such as PSNR, SSIM, FSIM, and MSE.

datasets_used.name: Not specified
datasets_used.description: The paper does not explicitly name the specific dataset, cohort, or image collection used for the experiments. It refers generally to 'skin cancer images' and 'images' in the context of evaluating the segmentation algorithm.

use.testing: The images were used to evaluate the segmentation performance of the proposed IGJO algorithm against seven other meta-heuristic algorithms using metrics PSNR, SSIM, FSIM, and MSE.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper compares algorithm performance using deterministic metrics (PSNR, SSIM, FSIM, MSE) without statistical significance tests.

skin_cancer_images.notes: The paper states that the proposed IGJO algorithm outperforms the seven compared meta-heuristic algorithms in terms of PSNR, SSIM, FSIM, and MSE segmentation metrics. However, specific numerical values for these metrics are not provided in the supplied text context.

conclusions: The proposed improved golden jackal optimization (IGJO) algorithm effectively resolves the multilevel thresholding problem for skin cancer image segmentation.

The IGJO algorithm outperforms seven other meta-heuristic algorithms (whale optimization, seagull optimization, salp swarm, Harris hawks optimization, artificial gorilla troops optimizer, marine predators’ algorithm, and original GJO) in terms of PSNR, SSIM, FSIM, and MSE segmentation metrics.
The efficient segmentation provided by the IGJO algorithm supports the improvement of the subsequent skin cancer classification process.
important_remarks: The specific dataset, cohort, or image collection used for experiments is not explicitly named in the provided text, referring only generally to 'skin cancer images'.
The paper does not report specific numerical values for the performance metrics (PSNR, SSIM, FSIM, MSE), only stating that the proposed IGJO algorithm 'outperforms' other algorithms.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is reported; performance comparison relies on deterministic metric values.
The study focuses on the optimization algorithm (IGJO) for segmentation rather than the final clinical classification task, limiting direct assessment of diagnostic accuracy.
External validation issues are present as the paper does not mention validation on independent or external datasets beyond the internal comparison of algorithms.
